title: Lesson 6: The Biblical Storyline
description: The Bible is not a collection of disconnected stories. It is one unified story told in four movements: Creation, Fall, Redemption, Restoration. And Jesus is the hero of every part. This lesson shows you the shape of that story, reveals how the same pattern repeats in almost every story in Scripture, and helps you see your own life inside the same four movements.

blocks.text: In the last lesson you explored the Bible as a library, 66 books arranged across two testaments. Now you are going to follow the line that runs through the whole library: one story from creation to new creation.

blocks.alt: A four-part sketch sequence showing the biblical story line from creation in a garden, to the fall, to redemption at the cross, to restoration in a renewed kingdom

blocks.title: The Story Line
blocks.text:
The Bible opens in a garden, and the line of the story begins with God. He made the heavens and the earth, filled the world with beauty, and placed Adam and Eve at the center of it as His image-bearers. They were made to walk with Him, enjoy His presence, and care for His world without shame or fear. This is Creation: everything good, everything whole, everything under the loving rule of God.

Then the serpent lied. He told them they could be like God without trusting God, and they believed him. They reached for the fruit, sin entered the world, and the garden became a place of hiding. The line dropped into brokenness, where their relationship with God was fractured and death entered what God had made alive. This is the Fall: sin breaking what God made good.

But God did not abandon His world. Even in the garden, He promised that one day a Son would come to crush the serpent and rescue His people. That promise carried through the whole Bible until Jesus came. At the cross, Jesus took the curse of sin. At the empty tomb, He defeated death. This is Redemption: God coming to rescue what people could never repair on their own.

And the story is not over. Jesus is coming again to make all things new. The Bible that begins in a garden ends in a new kingdom, where God dwells with His people and every tear is wiped away. This is Restoration: everything broken made whole again forever.
One Story in Four Movements

That is the storyline of the whole Bible: Creation, Fall, Redemption, and Restoration. The line begins with what God made good, drops when sin breaks the world, turns at the cross, and rises toward the day God makes all things new.
Use the interactive tool below to trace that line through the Bible's big story, through several smaller Bible stories, and then through your own life.

The whole Bible moves along one story line. Creation shows what God made good, the Fall shows how sin broke it, Redemption shows God coming to rescue through Jesus, and Restoration shows where the story is headed. The voice from the throne in the very last book of the Bible names the shape of the whole storyline in seven words.
Behold, I am making all things new.
6.1 - As you walked through the widget, which of the four movements (Creation, Fall, Redemption, Restoration) is sitting with you most right now? What in your life is making that movement feel close?

The parable and image sequence gave you the big picture first. Now the goal is to see the same pattern inside smaller Bible stories. As you read each one, look for what was good, what broke, how God stepped in, and what new beginning He brought.
The Pattern That Repeats

Here is what makes this framework so powerful: it is not just the shape of the Bible's big story. It is the shape of almost every story inside the Bible. The same four movements, creation, fall, redemption, restoration, show up again and again, like echoes of the one story God has been telling from the beginning. Once you see this pattern, you will find it everywhere.

1. Noah lived in a world God made good, but humanity corrupted it so deeply that God grieved He had made them. The flood was judgment, but the ark was rescue. And when the waters receded, God set a rainbow in the sky and made a covenant to start again. Creation, fall, redemption, restoration.

2. Joseph was a young man with dreams and a father's favor, until his brothers sold him into slavery and he spent years in a foreign prison. But God raised him from the pit to the palace, and the day came when Joseph could look at the brothers who betrayed him and say, "You meant it for evil, but God meant it for good." Creation, fall, redemption, restoration.

3. Israel was God's chosen people, but they ended up enslaved in Egypt for four hundred years, crying out for a deliverer. God sent Moses, poured out plagues on their captors, parted the sea, and led them out through blood and water into the land He had promised. Creation, fall, redemption, restoration.

4. Paul was brilliant, trained in Scripture, and burning with zeal for God, until that zeal turned murderous and he began hunting the very people God loved. Jesus met him on the road to Damascus and turned his life inside out. The man who destroyed the church became its greatest builder. Creation, fall, redemption, restoration.

Four stories. Four different centuries. Four completely different people. But the same line every time: something good, something broken, God stepping in, and a new beginning that is better than what was lost. This is not a coincidence. This is God's signature. He writes the same rescue into every chapter because He is the same rescuer in every chapter.
6.2 - Which of these four stories (Noah, Joseph, Moses, Paul) do you connect with most? What part of their storyline reminds you of something in your own life?
Your Story on the Line

And now, your story. You were made in the image of God with purpose, dignity, and worth that was settled before you ever did a single thing, and that is your creation. Then something broke, sin entered your life, and what was meant to be whole was fractured in your relationships, in your heart, and in the distance you felt from God, and that is your fall. But Jesus came for you, found you, forgave you, and gave you new life, and that is your redemption. And now you are living in the fourth movement, being restored day by day, not yet finished but already held. The same pattern that runs through the Bible is running through your life right now.

This means you are living between Redemption and Restoration. Jesus has won the victory, but you are still waiting for Him to return and make all things new. That is why life can feel confusing, with moments of breathtaking goodness reminding you of the way things were meant to be, and moments of deep pain reminding you the world is not yet what it will be. Both are real, and neither gets the last word. The last word belongs to the One who said, "Behold, I am making all things new."

6.3 - Try mapping your own story onto the four movements. What was your creation (the good things God built into your life)? What was your fall (where things broke)? What was your redemption (how Jesus found you)? And where do you see restoration happening right now?

6.4 - This is your private rehearsal before you take it out into the week. In your own words, how would you explain the four-movement story of the Bible to a friend in two or three minutes? Write it out here, just for yourself. You will use what you write below.
A Resource to Go Deeper

If this lesson sparked something in you and you want to see the biblical storyline explained visually, The Bible Project has a five-minute video that gives a beautiful overview of how the entire Bible fits together as one unified story. It is one of the best introductions out there.

blocks.title: The Bible Project: The Story of the Bible
blocks.description: A five-minute visual overview of how the entire Bible fits together as one unified story.

blocks.text: This Week's Practice

Now take what you wrote in question 6.4 out into the week. First, pick one Bible story you already know, any story, and walk it through the four movements yourself. Where was creation in that story? Where did the fall happen? How did God step in to redeem? And what new beginning did the story end with? Then find one real person this week, a family member, a coworker, a friend over coffee, and tell them the storyline of the whole Bible in two or three minutes the way you rehearsed it above. You will own the shape the moment you say it out loud to someone whose face is in front of you.
Lesson 6 Review

The line that opened in a garden and bends through the cross is the same line still bending through your week. You have walked the four movements that hold the entire Bible together: Creation, Fall, Redemption, and Restoration. You have seen the same pattern repeat in Noah, Joseph, Moses, and Paul. And you have been invited to see your own life on the same line, with Jesus as the hero of every chapter. Carry this one sentence with you: Creation, Fall, Redemption, Restoration. The same line runs through the Bible, and the same line runs through me. In the next lesson, you will learn a deeper method for studying any passage of the Bible on your own, building on the foundation you have been laying since lesson 4 of the Starting course.
Closing Reflections
What is one key thing you learned from this lesson?
What is one step you are going to take this week?
What is a prayer request for this week?
